content:
You have been asked by the user to revise a table that you previously prepared. 
The user's revision instructions are: Do no refer to the 'revised table format' in your commentary.

Here is information on the original table prior to the revision:
analysis_index: 44
item_index: 0
Title: Annual Discharge Compliance Summary 2014-2018
Analysis comments: The revised table format clearly demonstrates compliance performance across all parameters for the five-year period. Flow rates (337-358 ML/d) remained consistently at 62-66% of the 540 ML/d limit. Ammonia annual mean shows excellent compliance in four of five years (0.0859-0.127 mg/L, representing 17-25% of the 0.5 mg/L limit), with a significant exceedance in 2017 at 1.32 mg/L (264% of limit) requiring investigation. Despite this, ammonia 90th percentiles remained well-controlled at 0.11-0.18 mg/L (6-9% of the 2.0 mg/L limit). BOD 90th percentile values consistently met the 10 mg/L limit at 3-4 mg/L (30-40% of limit), demonstrating robust biological treatment throughout the period. The 2017 ammonia exceedance represents the only compliance concern identified.
Table: 
Parameter,Discharge limit,2014,2015,2016,2017,2018
Flow Rate - Annual Mean (ML/d),540.0,337.0,343.0,358.0,354.0,343.0
Ammonia - Annual Mean (mg/L),0.5,0.0907,0.0859,0.0938,1.32,0.127
Ammonia - 90th Percentile (mg/L),2.0,0.132,0.11,0.13,0.13,0.18
BOD - 90th Percentile (mg/L),10.0,3.0,4.0,4.0,3.0,3.0


Code used to create table: 
# filename: revised_annual_discharge_compliance_summary.py
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Data for the table
years = ['2014', '2015', '2016', '2017', '2018']

# Original data from the user input
flow_rates = [337.405, 342.521, 358.068, 354.452, 343.107]
ammonia_annual_mean = [0.0907258, 0.0859339, 0.0938031, 1.32132, 0.127017]
ammonia_90th = [0.1315, 0.11, 0.13, 0.13, 0.18]
bod_90th = [3.0, 4.0, 4.0, 3.0, 3.0]

# Discharge limits
limits = {
    'Flow Rate - Annual Mean (ML/d)': 540,
    'Ammonia - Annual Mean (mg/L)': 0.5,
    'Ammonia - 90th Percentile (mg/L)': 2.0,
    'BOD - 90th Percentile (mg/L)': 10.0,
}

# Function to round to 3 significant figures
def round_sig(x, sig=3):
    if x == 0:
        return 0
    return round(x, sig - int(np.floor(np.log10(abs(x)))) - 1)

# Round the values as required
flow_rates_rounded = [round_sig(x, 3) for x in flow_rates]
ammonia_annual_mean_rounded = [round_sig(x, 3) for x in ammonia_annual_mean]
ammonia_90th_rounded = [round_sig(x, 3) for x in ammonia_90th]

# BOD values remain as integers
bod_90th_int = [int(round(x)) for x in bod_90th]

# Construct the dataframe with parameters as rows
data = {
    'Parameter': ['Flow Rate - Annual Mean (ML/d)',
                  'Ammonia - Annual Mean (mg/L)',
                  'Ammonia - 90th Percentile (mg/L)',
                  'BOD - 90th Percentile (mg/L)'],
    'Discharge limit': [limits['Flow Rate - Annual Mean (ML/d)'],
                        limits['Ammonia - Annual Mean (mg/L)'],
                        limits['Ammonia - 90th Percentile (mg/L)'],
                        limits['BOD - 90th Percentile (mg/L)']],
    '2014': [flow_rates_rounded[0],
             ammonia_annual_mean_rounded[0],
             ammonia_90th_rounded[0],
             bod_90th_int[0]],
    '2015': [flow_rates_rounded[1],
             ammonia_annual_mean_rounded[1],
             ammonia_90th_rounded[1],
             bod_90th_int[1]],
    '2016': [flow_rates_rounded[2],
             ammonia_annual_mean_rounded[2],
             ammonia_90th_rounded[2],
             bod_90th_int[2]],
    '2017': [flow_rates_rounded[3],
             ammonia_annual_mean_rounded[3],
             ammonia_90th_rounded[3],
             bod_90th_int[3]],
    '2018': [flow_rates_rounded[4],
             ammonia_annual_mean_rounded[4],
             ammonia_90th_rounded[4],
             bod_90th_int[4]],
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Ensure columns order
df = df[['Parameter', 'Discharge limit', '2014', '2015', '2016', '2017', '2018']]

# Save the final processed table to CSV
output_csv = 'revised_annual_discharge_compliance_summary.csv'
df.to_csv(output_csv, index=False)

print(output_csv)

This table has been produced from source data as follows:
File name: MWC_ETP_Data.xlsx
Data description: This file contains operational data from a municipal wastewater effluent treatment plant (ETP) covering a five-year period from 2014 to 2018. The dataset includes daily influent flow rates, influent and effluent water quality parameters (including ammonia, BOD, COD, nitrogen species), daily electricity consumption and generation data, and regulatory discharge limits. The data supports performance monitoring, compliance assessment, and operational analysis of the treatment facility.
Sheet name: ETP Influent Flow
Headings: Date, Influent Flow (ML/d)
Time period: 2014-01-01 to 2018-12-31
Time step: 1
Time format: Daily

Sheet name: ETP Influent Quality
Headings: Date, Ammonia (mg/L), BOD (mg/L), COD (mg/L), Nitrate plus Nitrite (mg/L), Total Nitrogen (mg/L)
Time period: 2014-01-01 to 2018-12-20
Time step: Variable (irregular sampling)
Time format: Daily

Sheet name: ETP Effluent Quality
Headings: Date, COD (mg/L), BOD (mg/L), Ammonia (mg/L), Total Kjeldahl Nitrogen (mg/L), Total Nitrogen (mg/L), Nitrate plus Nitrite (mg/L)
Time period: 2014-01-01 to 2018-12-31
Time step: Variable (irregular sampling)
Time format: Daily

Sheet name: ETP Electricity Usage
Headings: Date, Main  feeder 1 (kwh/d), Main  feeder 2 (kwh/d), Digester Gas Electricity Generation (kWh/d)
Time period: 2014-01-01 to 2018-12-31
Time step: 1
Time format: Daily

Sheet name: Discharge Limits
Headings: Parameter, Discharge Limit, Units
Time period: Not applicable to Not applicable
Time step: Not applicable
Time format: Not applicable

Other Charts and Tables:
For your reference, here are the findings from other charts and tables that have been created from this dataset. You will not be revising these.
Title: Annual Mean Flow Rate vs Discharge Limit 2014-2018
Comments: The annual mean flow rates show excellent compliance with the 540 ML/d discharge limit throughout the five-year period. Flow rates ranged from 337 ML/d (2014) to 358 ML/d (2016), representing 62-66% of the permitted limit. This substantial margin below the limit indicates the plant has significant hydraulic capacity for future growth. A slight upward trend is visible from 2014 to 2016 (6% increase), with flows stabilizing in 2017-2018. The consistent performance well below the limit demonstrates reliable flow management.

Title: Annual Ammonia Mean and 90th Percentile vs Discharge Limits 2014-2018
Comments: Effluent ammonia performance shows excellent compliance for four of five years, with a significant exceedance in 2017. Annual mean ammonia remained well below the 0.5 mg/L limit for 2014-2016 and 2018 (0.09-0.13 mg/L), but exceeded in 2017 at 1.32 mg/L (264% of limit). The 90th percentile values consistently remained excellent at 0.11-0.18 mg/L (6-9% of 2.0 mg/L limit) across all years. The 2017 anomaly in the annual mean, despite acceptable 90th percentile performance, suggests intermittent rather than sustained nitrification issues, potentially due to operational upsets, temperature effects, or process control problems requiring investigation.

Title: Annual BOD 90th Percentile vs Discharge Limit 2014-2018
Comments: Effluent BOD 90th percentile values demonstrate excellent and consistent compliance with the 10 mg/L discharge limit throughout the five-year period. Values ranged from 3-4 mg/L (30-40% of limit), indicating robust secondary treatment performance with substantial safety margin. The slight elevation in 2015-2016 (4 mg/L) compared to 2014, 2017, and 2018 (3 mg/L) represents normal operational variability and remains well within compliance. This consistently low BOD performance reflects effective biological treatment and process stability across all years.

Title: Daily Discharge Flow 2014-2018
Comments: Daily flow data reveals significant variability with occasional high-flow events throughout the monitoring period. Baseline flows typically range between 250-450 ML/d, with periodic wet-weather spikes reaching 650-850 ML/d. Notable extreme events occurred in October 2016 (850 ML/d), August 2017 (790 ML/d), and January 2018 (1,330 ML/d), with the latter representing a major storm event exceeding twice the normal flow. Despite these peaks, all flows remained below the 540 ML/d annual mean limit when averaged. Several anomalous negative and very low flow values in early 2014 suggest data quality issues requiring validation. The wet-weather response highlights the plant's hydraulic capacity to handle storm flows while maintaining treatment performance.

Title: Daily Effluent Ammonia 2014-2018
Comments: Effluent ammonia shows generally excellent performance with concentrations typically below 0.5 mg/L (annual mean limit) throughout most of the monitoring period. However, several significant nitrification failures are evident: a catastrophic event in mid-2017 reaching 316 mg/L (not shown on scale), multiple excursions to 3-10 mg/L in mid-2017 and throughout 2018, and isolated spikes in early 2014. The 2017 failures explain the elevated annual mean (1.32 mg/L) observed in the compliance summary. These upsets likely indicate operational issues such as dissolved oxygen deficiency, temperature impacts, toxic shock loads, or insufficient biomass. The pattern suggests persistent nitrification challenges requiring process optimization, particularly during 2017-2018.

Title: Daily Effluent BOD 2014-2018
Comments: Effluent BOD demonstrates excellent compliance with the 10 mg/L (90th percentile) discharge limit throughout the entire monitoring period. The majority of measurements are at or near the detection limit of 2 mg/L, indicating highly effective organic matter removal. Occasional elevated values of 3-6 mg/L appear scattered across all years, representing normal operational variability but remaining well below the limit. One notable excursion occurred in January 2015 with a brief spike to 5 mg/L, possibly correlating with process upsets. The consistent low BOD performance across five years confirms robust secondary biological treatment, stable process control, and adequate treatment capacity, with no compliance concerns identified.
